notes.note: Hysteron proteron, a Greek rhetorical term for reversing the natural order of two items. Cicero jokes that he will answer Atticus's second question first.
The auctoritas senatus: the moral and political weight of the senate's collective will, which Cicero presents himself as defending against Clodius's appeals to the popular assemblies.
The special-court bill set up to try Clodius for violating the December 62 BC rites of Bona Dea, which he had infiltrated disguised as a woman. The dispute was over how the jury would be empanelled.

The original bill, backed by the consuls and the optimates, would have let the praetor hand-pick the jury, ensuring conviction. Fufius's substitute bill restored the ordinary lot-and-rejection method, opening the way to bribery.
Senatus consultum (SC): a formal resolution of the senate. A tribune could veto (intercessio) the legislation it recommended, which is the obstruction Hortensius feared from Fufius.
A proverb for an easy kill: even a blunt lead blade would do. Hortensius was so sure of conviction he accepted the weaker jury procedure.

The equites (Roman knights) were one of the three jury classes after Pompey's settlement; Cicero sneers that these particular knights were destitute, hence open to bribes. The jury panel mixed senators, equites, and tribuni aerarii.
A pun on the tribuni aerarii, the third jury class: Cicero twists aerati ("of the treasury," i.e. moneyed) into aerarii to brand them paupers fit for bribery.
Q. Caecilius Metellus Numidicus, the upright consul of 109 BC whose written deposition jurors once refused to read out of respect. Cicero cites it as a precedent for the honour shown him here.
Cicero jokingly calls the honest jurors who escorted him home "Areopagites," after the venerable Athenian homicide court on the Areopagus, a byword for incorruptible justice.
A coded slur for M. Licinius Crassus, who organized the jury bribery: "Calvus" (baldhead) plus a reference to property he had bought up in the Sullan proscriptions of one Nanneius.
The consul Piso (M. Pupius Piso) expected Syria as his proconsular province; Cicero boasts of having maneuvered to deny it to him as punishment for mishandling the Clodius affair.
The Ides fell on the 15th of March, May, July, and October, the 13th of other months; here 15 May 61 BC, the senate session at which Cicero confronted Clodius.
In operto, "in the secret place": Cicero turns Clodius's jibe about Baiae into a barbed reminder of the Bona Dea rites Clodius profaned, which were strictly forbidden to men.

notes.anchor: Rex made no mention of you
notes.note: A double pun: Cicero denies being a "king" (rex) while noting that Q. Marcius Rex, Clodius's brother-in-law, left him nothing in his will, an inheritance Clodius had counted on.
"Magnus" (the Great) was Pompey's cognomen. Cicero plays on it, half-mockingly noting the cooling between them even as the urban plebs supposed him Pompey's intimate.
L. Afranius, Pompey's loyal lieutenant, whom Pompey was pushing (with bribery) into the consulship of 60 BC. "Auli filius" hints at his undistinguished birth.
The proverb attributed to Philip II of Macedon: no fortress is impregnable if an ass loaded with gold can reach its gate. Cicero applies it to Pompey's electoral bribery.
Divisores: men who distributed bribe-money to voters tribe by tribe, a recognized (if illegal) electoral profession at Rome.

notes.anchor: on the proposal of Cato and Domitius
notes.note: Two anti-bribery senatus consulta proposed by Cato the Younger and L. Domitius Ahenobarbus, aimed at the consul thought to be shielding the divisores in his house.

The leges Aelia et Fufia regulated obstruction of legislation by religious means (obnuntiatio). Lurco was exempted from them so his anti-bribery bill could proceed despite his entering office under a disqualifying technicality.
Sextilis was the sixth month (later renamed August); its Kalends fell on 1 August. "The sixth day before" by inclusive Roman reckoning is 27 July.
Three thousand sesterces, the fine the new bribery law set per tribesman for a candidate who paid out after naming a price. HS is the standard sign for the sestertius.
Fabam mimum: a low farce; the bean was a stock prop of the popular mime stage. Cicero fears his proud consulship will be reduced to a vulgar joke if Clodius's man wins.
Atticus's private shrine and garden retreat at his Buthrotum estate in Epirus, named for the nymph Amalthea and adorned with epigrams; Cicero wants to build one at Arpinum.
The nymph (or she-goat) who nursed the infant Zeus; her name evokes abundance (the cornucopia), suiting a leisured rural shrine.
